coredata.dc:title: Thin-film nanocomposite nanofiltration membrane with enhanced desalination and antifouling performance via incorporating L-aspartic acid functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Chenpu
Xie, Quanling
Hong, Zhuan
Shen, Lufang
Yu, Tong
Guo, Honghui
Xiong, Ying
Zhang, Guoliang
Lu, Yinghua
Shao, Wenyao
coredata.dc:description: To fabricate thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) nanofiltration (NF) membrane with enhanced desalination and antifouling performance, L-aspartic acid functionalized graphene quantum dots (AGQDs), integrating the advantages of L-aspartic acid and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), were successfully synthesized and incorporated into the polyamide (PA) selective layer via interfacial polymerization (IP) method. The fabricated TFN membranes with various AGQDs contents were systematically characterized by the microstructures, surface properties, desalination, and antifouling performance. The optimal AGQDs concentration in the aqueous solution reaches 3000 ppm, the resultant TFN-3 membrane exhibits an outstanding water permeance of 18.5 LMH/bar, 60.9% higher than the TFC-blank membrane. Meanwhile, the TFN-3 membrane possesses a higher Na2SO4 rejection than that of the TFC-blank membrane (98.4% vs 95.7%). Additionally, the TFN-3 membrane shows superior antifouling performance over the TFC-blank membrane in the presence of either the negatively charged foulant (BSA) or the positively charged foulant (lysozyme). An appropriate amount of AGQDs added into aqueous solution favors the formation of a smooth, thin, hydrophilic, and electrically neutral selective layer. As a result, the overall separation performance of the TFN-3 membrane, especially the desalination and antifouling performance, are significantly enhanced with the help of AGQDs. Therefore, the incorporation of amino acid-functionalized GQDs into the PA selective layer presents a novel strategy to produce a high-performance NF membrane for water desalination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofiltration
Graphene quantum dots
L-aspartic acid
Thin-film nanocomposite
Desalination

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the synthetic process of AGQDs.
The fabricated process of NF membranes.

TEM image and the corresponding size distribution (A), 2D AFM image and height profile (B), FT-IR spectra (C), XPS C1spectrum (D), XRD pattern (E), zeta potentials (F), fluorescent images (G) of AGQDs.
ATR-FTIR spectra of fabricated membranes (A), XPS full spectra of fabricated membranes (B), fitting curve spectra of C1s of TFC-blank membrane (C), and TFN-3 membrane (D).
Raman spectra (A) and zeta potentials (B) of TFC-blank and TFN-3 membranes; fluorescence images of TFC-blank (C) and TFN-3 (D) membranes.
SEM images of fabricated membranes: (A) TFC-blank, (B) TFN-1, (C) TFN-2, (D) TFN-3, (E) TFN-4.
Cross-sectional SEM images of fabricated membranes: (A) TFC-blank, (B) TFN-1, (C) TFN-2, (D) TFN-3, (E) TFN-4.
Two- and three-dimensional AFM surface images of the fabricated membranes: (A) TFC-blank, (B) TFN-1, (C) TFN-2, (D) TFN-3, (E) TFN-4.
Water flux (A) and salt rejection (B) of fabricated NF membranes as a function of AGQDs concentration in aqueous solutions. Testing conditions: salts concentration = 2000 ppm, 0.55 MPa, 25 °C.

Time-dependent normalized flux of the TFC-blank and TFN-3 membranes by filtrating BSA (A) and lysozyme (B). Testing conditions: BSA concentration = 2000 ppm, lysozyme concentration = 2000 ppm, 0.55 MPa, 25 °C.
Comparisons of water permeance and Na2SO4 rejection between the TFN-3 membrane and the NF membranes in literature. The detailed testing conditions in Table S3.
The critical parameter of different salts ions.
The chemical compositions of NF membranes obtained from XPS spectra.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: O/N: molar ratio of nitrogen to carbon in the selective layers.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: O/C: molar ratio of oxygen to carbon in the selective layers.

head.ce:title: Thin-film nanocomposite nanofiltration membrane with enhanced desalination and antifouling performance via incorporating L-aspartic acid functionalized graphene quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Innovation Center for Exploitation of Marine Biological Resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To fabricate thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) nanofiltration (NF) membrane with enhanced desalination and antifouling performance, L-aspartic acid functionalized graphene quantum dots (AGQDs), integrating the advantages of L-aspartic acid and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), were successfully synthesized and incorporated into the polyamide (PA) selective layer via interfacial polymerization (IP) method. The fabricated TFN membranes with various AGQDs contents were systematically characterized by the microstructures, surface properties, desalination, and antifouling performance. The optimal AGQDs concentration in the aqueous solution reaches 3000 ppm, the resultant TFN-3 membrane exhibits an outstanding water permeance of 18.5 LMH/bar, 60.9% higher than the TFC-blank membrane. Meanwhile, the TFN-3 membrane possesses a higher Na2SO4 rejection than that of the TFC-blank membrane (98.4% vs 95.7%). Additionally, the TFN-3 membrane shows superior antifouling performance over the TFC-blank membrane in the presence of either the negatively charged foulant (BSA) or the positively charged foulant (lysozyme). An appropriate amount of AGQDs added into aqueous solution favors the formation of a smooth, thin, hydrophilic, and electrically neutral selective layer. As a result, the overall separation performance of the TFN-3 membrane, especially the desalination and antifouling performance, are significantly enhanced with the help of AGQDs. Therefore, the incorporation of amino acid-functionalized GQDs into the PA selective layer presents a novel strategy to produce a high-performance NF membrane for water desalination.

ce:section.ce:para: Thin-film composite (TFC) nanofiltration (NF) has advantages of low energy consumption, high separation efficiency, and being environment-friendly [1]. Therefore, it has received increasing research interest as a robust method of water desalination. However, NF still encounters serious challenges, including membrane fouling and the trade-off effect between flux and rejection [2]. Various nanofillers have been extensively incorporated into the polyamide (PA) selective layer to overcome these challenges [3–6].

The two-dimensional graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets have extra nanochannels with excellent mechanical stability and chemical inertness [7–9]. It is a great candidate to produce the high-performance thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) NF membranes. Hu et al. [10] fabricated a highly wrinkled surface and ultrafast water channels centred PA membrane by introducing GO during the interfacial polymerization (IP) process, and the fabricated membranes present sandwich structures and extraordinary high water flux as well as maintain constant salt rejection. Compared with carbon nanotubes or graphene, GO nanosheets show better dispersibility and compatibility with the IP conditions owing to the existence of oxygen-containing groups on their basal plane and edge. In fact, GO is a kind of amphiphilic nanoparticles having the hydrophilic edge and the hydrophobic center [11]. Thus excessive GO leads to uneven dispersion and severe aggregation within the PA layer, which limits its potential to manipulate the structure and property of the PA selective layer.

The zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are typically ranged from 3 to 20 nm having abundant hydrophilic groups. GQDs clusters successfully overcome the aggregation caused by the strong intermolecular force between graphene sheets. Hence GQDs exhibit superior water dispersibility, IP compatibility, and polymer affinity over GO nanosheets. Recently, the GQDs-incorporated membrane has become an attractive way to enhance membrane performance. Jiang's research group [12] used GQDs as the aqueous monomers and directly reacted with trimesoyl chloride (TMC) to reconstruct the pores of ultrafiltration (UF) membranes. The GQDs were fixed within the UF pores due to the irreversible thermal shrinkage process, which contributes to high water flux and high rejection of Alcian blue and Congo red. They also used GQDs as the aqueous nanofillers to tune the surface roughness and hydrophilicity [13]. Sun et al. [14] systematically investigated three functionalized GQDs with carboxyl, amino and sulfonic acid groups on the NF properties and performance. The results revealed that the separation performance of TFN NF membranes was firmly related to the functional groups of GQDs.

Many studies reveal that the zwitterionic membranes demonstrate improved antifouling performance due to the zwitterionic pairs on membrane surfaces [15–17]. Amino acids are known as zwitterionic compounds because of the coexistence of amine and carboxyl groups. L-aspartic acid (L-Asp) is a natural amino acid containing two carboxyl groups and an amine group. Besides the zwitterionic feature, L-Asp is widely used as the functionalized amino acid due to its outstanding hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, nontoxicity, and low cost [18–20].

In this study, the L-Asp functionalized graphene quantum dots (AGQDs), combining the advantages of GQDs and L-Asp, were synthesized and incorporated into the PA selective layer to enhance desalination and antifouling performance of NF membrane. AGQDs have two major superiorities over the pristine GQDs. On the one hand, the introduction of L-Asp endows the zwitterionic feature of AGQDs, which is expected to improve the membrane's fouling resistance. On the other hand, AGQDs have better dispersibility and affinity on the polysulfone (PSU) support, facilitating more AGQDs engaged in the IP process [18,21]. It could maximize the nanofiller's contributions to the structure and performance of the fabricated NF membrane. Herein we systematically investigated the effects of AGQDs concentration on the microstructures, surface properties, desalination, and antifouling properties of TFN NF membranes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to utilize amino acid-functionalized GQDs to construct a high-performance TFN NF membrane.

Citric acid (98% purity) and bovine serum albumin (BSA, 99% purity) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin (China). L-aspartic acid (>98% purity) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Caustic soda (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37% purity), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99% purity), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, 99% purity), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5% purity), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 98% purity) and hexane (97% purity) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (China). Piperazine (PIP, 99% purity) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98% purity) were produced by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. BSA and lysozyme was purchased from Shanghai BBI Life Sciences Corp. (China).

AGQDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal treatment, as depicted in Fig. 1 [22–24]. In brief, 10 g citric acid (CA) and 5 g L-Asp were mixed and dissolved in 50 mL deionized water (DI) at the same time. Then the mixture was continuously stirred by a magnetic heating agitator for 2 h (800 rpm, 200 °C). The dissolved CA reacted with L-Asp during the heating procedure. Then the mixed liquid gradually changed from colorless to red-orange, indicating the formation of raw AGQDs. Subsequently, the obtained solution was neutralized with 1 mol·L−1 NaOH. The neutralized solution was purified by 1 kDa dialysis membrane for 1 day. Finally, the purified solution was concentrated by a rotating evaporator and dried in a vacuum drying oven.

The fabrication process of NF membranes is illustrated in Fig. 2 . The lab-made PSU support [25] was kept in deionized water for 30 min before use. The PSU support was fixed on the homemade polypropylene frame. AGQDs nanofillers were added into the aqueous phase containing 0.1 wt% PIP, which were treated by ultrasonic dispersion for 1 h to obtain a homogeneous aqueous solution. The as-prepared aqueous solution was slowly poured onto the surface of the PSU support and kept for 20 min. The resulting PSU support was dried in the air after draining off the excessive aqueous solution. Subsequently, 0.1 w/v% TMC organic solution was further poured onto the surface of the PIP/AGQDs saturated PSU support and removed after 2 min. Finally, the nascent composite membrane was heated at 60 °C for 20 min. The fabricated PA membrane without AGQDs was named TFC-blank. The fabricated TFN membrane was named TFN-x, where x denoted the aqueous solution containing x thousand ppm of AGQDs.

The chemical property of AGQDs was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker VERTXE 70, Switzerland). Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 1400, Japan) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension Edge, Bruker, Germany) were utilized to observe the size and structure of AGQDs. The chemical composition was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Physical Instruments Quantum 2000, USA). The fluorescent properties of AGQDs were imaged by the fluorescent microscope (DM6 B, Leica, Germany). Raman spectroscopy (Thermo Renishaw, USA) was used to observe the sp2 and sp3 graphitic structures. The crystal morphology was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Discover, Germany). The average size distribution and the zeta potential were confirmed by a particle-size analyzer (Nano Zetasizer 90, Malvern Instruments, UK).

Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Bruker Vertex 70, Switzerland) was used to detect the functional groups of the selective layers. The chemical compositions of the selective layers were measured by XPS (Physical Instruments Quantum 2000, USA). The hydrophilic property of the membrane surface was confirmed by using a contact angle goniometer (Beijing HARKE SPCAX3, China). The fluorescent images were taken by fluorescent microscopy under different excitation wavelengths. The surface and cross-section morphologies of the selected membranes were observed by SEM (LEO-1530, Germany) operated at 10 kV and 20 kV, respectively. The surface roughness was observed by atomic force microscopy with a C3000 controller (AFM, Nanosurf FlexAFM, Switzerland) in scanning mode with a scanning range of 10 μm × 10 μm. The membranes surface zeta potential was measured by a SurPASS electrokinetic analyzer installed with a sample cell (Anton Paar GmbH, Austria).

A cross-flow membrane separation equipment tested the fabricated NF membranes with an active filtration area of 46 cm2 under the pressure of 0.55 MPa at 25 °C. Four different salts were used to evaluate the overall rejection rate of fabricated NF membranes with the salt concentration of 2000 ppm. The critical parameters of different ions are listed in Table 1 .[
14
,
26
]

The water flux and salt rejection were calculated by the following Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively:
(1)
J =
V AΔt
where J is the water flux (L·m−2·h−1, abbreviated as LMH), V is the permeate volume (L), A represents the active filtration area (m2), and △t is the running time (h).
(2)
R
%
=
1 −
c P
c c
× 100 %
where C
p and C
c represent the salt concentration of the permeate and the concentrate, respectively. The conductivity meter (Mettler Toledo S3, Switzerland) was used to determine the salt concentration based on the standard curve between the conductivity and the salt concentration.

BSA and lysozyme were used as the model foulants to evaluate the antifouling performance of the selected NF membranes. First, the pure water flux was tested for 60 min at 0.55 MPa and recorded every 10 min. Second, the permeate flux of foulant-contained solution was measured under the same operating conditions. After water washing, the procedures mentioned above were repeated.

The ATR-FTIR was employed to analyze the chemical compositions of the fabricated membranes, as shown in Fig. 4A. The peak at 1646 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1 are attributed to the CO and NH bending vibration, respectively. It means the successful formation of the PA selective layer. [28,35,36]. Compared with the TFC-blank membrane, the broad bands at around 3410 cm−1 are slightly intensified for the TFN membranes, which are ascribed to the incorporation of hydrophilic AGQDs [28]. Nevertheless, ATR-FTIR is not sensitive enough to detect the difference between the TFC-blank and TFN membranes. Therefore, XPS was further conducted to identify the elemental compositions of the fabricated NF membranes.

The XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 4(B-D), and the different atomic concentrations are listed in Table 2 . Both the TFC-blank and TFN NF membranes consist of C, O, and N elements, which is also ascribed to the newly formed PA selective layer on PSU support. For the polypiperazineamide TFC membrane (Fig. S1 and Table S1), the N and O elements come from the aqueous PIP monomers and the organic TMC monomers, respectively. Thus the cross-linking degree of the PA layer could be calculated from the N and O atomic percentages [37]. Commonly, higher O/N and O/C ratios indicate a lower cross-linking degree of the PA layer. For the TFN membranes in this study, the embedded AGQDs also provide extra N and O elements (Table S2). So the cross-linking degree of the nanocomposite selective layer cannot be directly calculated from the N and O contents. More importantly, the introduced AGQDs have two opposite effects on the cross-linking degree of the selective layer. 1) During the impregnated stage of aqueous solution, more PIP monomers saturate and remain on PSU support owing to the hydrogen bond interaction and electrostatic attraction between PIP and AGQDs. Thus more PIP monomers are available for the IP reaction, which increases the cross-linking degree of the selective layer. 2) During the stage of IP reaction, AGQDs hinder the diffusion rate of PIP monomers due to the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction between PIP and AGQDs [38]. Thus less PIP monomers are involved in the IP reaction, which accordingly decreases the cross-linking degree of the selective layer. Therefore, the cross-linking degree of the selective layer is closely related to the AGQDs content. It's worth noting that the TFN-4 membrane displays the remarkable higher O/N and O/C ratios compared with the other TFN membranes. It is deduced that excessive AGQDs significantly reduce the cross-linking degree of the PA layer, which is confirmed by the decreased salt rejections (Fig. 10B).

As mentioned above, AGQDs with abundant oxygen-containing groups are nonnegligible for the oxygen content of the selective layer. Although the TFN membranes present the higher O/N ratios compared with the TFC-blank membrane, it doesn't mean the lower cross-linking degree of the nanocomposite selective layer. Except for the TFN-4 membrane, the other TFN membranes exhibit higher salt rejections than the TFC-blank membrane (Fig. 10B). It implies that their higher O/N ratios are mainly ascribed to the oxygen-containing groups in AGQDs rather than the hydrolysis of excess acyl chloride groups. Notably, the TFN-1 membrane shows a higher O/N ratio but a lower O/C ratio than the TFC-blank membrane, which also proves the formation of the denser selective layer with the help of AGQDs.

Fig. 4C and D show the fitting curve of the C1s spectrum of the TFC-blank membrane and the TFN-3 membrane, respectively. Both membranes show the equivalent peak intensity at 287.6 eV attributed to the N-C=O/O-C=O groups [23,32]. Although the N-C=O mostly generates from the IP reaction between PIP and TMC, the introduction of AGQDs in aqueous solution slows the diffusion rate of PIP monomers. However, AGQDs with abundant oxygen-containing groups compensate for the intensity reduction of the N-C=O peak. As a result, the N-C=O/O-C=O peak remains unchanged in the C1s spectrum.

The Raman spectra and zeta potentials of the selected membranes are illustrated in Fig. 5A and B. As for the Raman spectra, no characteristic peaks of AGQDs (G peak of 1350 cm−1 and D peak of 1590 cm−1) appear in the TFN-3 membrane, probably due to the substantial fluorescent property of AGQDs [36]. It is commonly believed that the negatively charged PA TFC membranes originate from the hydrolysis of unreacted acyl chloride groups from TMC. Besides the successful introduction of zwitterionic AGQDs, the abundant active groups in AGQDs could react with TMC during IP process and consume a certain amount of acyl chloride groups from TMC [39]. As a result, the TFN-3 membrane tends to be electrically neutral (Fig. 5B), especially alleviating the negative charge while pH beyond 7 [40]. Because the isoelectric point of AGQDs is below 3, the TFN-3 membrane shows a lower isoelectric point than the TFC-blank membrane [41]. According to Fig. 5C and D, it is evident that the TFN-3 membrane displays the remarkable fluorescence feature compared with the TFC-blank membrane, which matches very well with the fluorescent property of AGQDs [42].

SEM was conducted to observe the surface morphologies of the fabricated membranes. Because TMC has minimal solubility in the aqueous phase, the IP reaction mostly depends on the diffusion rate of PIP monomers and takes place on the side of the organic phase. As shown in Fig. 6 , the TFC-blank membrane exhibits a rough but compact surface with a typical nodule structure. After adding AGQDs in the aqueous solution, the resultant TFN membranes demonstrate fewer nodule structures as the AGQDs hinder the diffusion of PIP monomers. In Zhang's study [38], they introduced macromolecular polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to control the diffusion rate of PIP into the organic phase via the strong hydrogen bonding between PVA and PIP and the increasing viscosity of the aqueous solution, which resulted in rougher and heterogeneous Turing structures. In this study, the diffusion behaviors of PIP monomers were affected by the introduction of AGQDs, which eventually altered the morphologies of the selective layer. Additionally, the tight-packed nodules structures in the TFN membranes are quite different from the TFC-blank membrane [43]. With the increasing concentration of AGQDs, the TFN membrane surface exhibits a relatively smooth and compact structure, which is firmly related to the reduced diffusion rate of PIP. The excellent polymer affinity characteristic enables a large amount of AGQDs to remain on the PSU support [44]. Under the combined interactions of electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bonding between PIP and AGQDs, this part of PIP monomers slowly diffuses towards the organic phase, which leads to form a relatively loose bottom selective layer that close to PSU support. Meanwhile, the other PIP monomers freely diffusing into the organic phase tend to form a smooth and non-defective top selective layer with high cross-linking degree (Fig. 2).

Fig. 7 shows the cross-sectional SEM morphologies of the fabricated membranes. The thickness of the selective layers gradually decreases with the increasing AGQDs concentration. The thickness decreases from 196.5 nm to 102.7 nm, which mainly results from the reduced rate of IP reaction after introducing AGQDs. IP is a reaction-diffusion process that the diffusion rate of aqueous monomer plays a crucial role in the microstructures of the PA layer [45]. With the addition of AGQDs in the aqueous phase, a lot of PIP monomers surround AGQDs due to the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction between PIP and AGQDs, which retards the free diffusion of PIP. Meanwhile, the diffusions of the other free PIP monomers without surrounding AGQDs are also affected by the steric hindrance of AGQDs. As a result, a thinner nanocomposite selective layer formed on the PSU support after introducing AGQDs in the aqueous phase.

According to Fig. 8 , the roughness of the fabricated NF membranes gradually decreases with the increasing AGQDs concentration. When the AGQDs concentration reaches 4000 ppm, the roughness decreases from 23.0 nm to 4.0 nm. The reduced roughness of membrane surfaces agrees well with the SEM observations, which has been well explained in the SEM section.

The water contact angle (WCA) is measured to evaluate the membrane surface hydrophilicity in the water-air interface. WCA depends upon surface hydrophilicity, roughness, porosity, pore size, and its distribution [46]. In terms of hydrophilicity, more hydrophilic AGQDs were embedded in the selective layer with increasing AGQDs concentration, which enhanced the hydrophilicity of the membrane surfaces. In terms of roughness, the membrane surfaces become smoother with increasing AGQDs concentration, which also increases the surface wettability to some extent and decreases the WCA. Additionally, the influences of pore size and porosity on WCA could be ignored for the NF membrane. As a result, the WCA presents a gradually declined trend with increasing AGQDs concentration (Fig. 9 ).

The desalination performance (Fig. 10 ) was measured under the trans-membrane pressure of 0.55 MPa at 25 °C. Apart from the TFN-4 membrane, the water fluxes of the other TFN membranes significantly increased with the increasing AGQDs concentration, while the Na2SO4 rejections maintain at a relatively stable and high level. Compared with the TFC-blank membrane (11.5 LMH/bar, 95.7%), the optimal TFN-3 membrane possesses a higher water flux of 18.5 LMH/bar and a higher Na2SO4 rejection of 98.4%. Namely, the optimized AGQDs-incorporated TFN membranes show the remarkably enhanced water permeance without sacrificing salt rejection. It suggests that the nanocomposite selective layer is still integrated and defect-free after incorporating AGQDs within the PA matrix. Compared with the pristine GQDs, the water flux of the GQDs-incorporated membrane improved significantly, but Na2SO4 rejection dramatically decreased from 90.2% to 40.6% [47]. In this study, an appropriate amount of AGQDs introduced in the aqueous solution not only favor the formation of the denser, thinner, smoother and stronger hydrophilic nanocomposite selective layer, but also create additional water channels within the selective layer [24]. Furthermore, the interface voids between AGQDs and PA matrix unavoidably exist, which offers extra flow paths for water molecules [48]. As a result, the optimal AGQDs-incorporated TFN NF membrane shows a superior overall desalination performance by overcoming the trade-off effect between water flux and salt rejection, which is attributed to the reduction of membrane filtration resistance, the enhancement of the surface hydrophilicity, and the formation of additional water channels.

The rejections of four different salts were measured to explore the selectivity and separation mechanism of the fabricated NF membranes. According to Fig. 10B, the different salt rejections followed the sequence of Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > MgCl2 > NaCl, which is consistent with the negatively charged NF membrane [49,50]. It's well known that the separation mechanism of NF is complicated but primarily attributed to the synergetic effects of size exclusion and Donnan exclusion. Generally, the negatively charged NF membrane has a higher rejection towards the salts with divalent anion (SO4 2−) compared with the salts with monovalent anion (Cl−), owing to the stronger electrostatic repulsion towards SO4 2− than Cl−. For the salts having the same divalent SO4 2−, MgSO4 rejection is much lower than Na2SO4 rejection because the negatively charged membrane surface also generates the stronger electrostatic attraction towards the divalent Mg2+ cation than the monovalent Na+ cation. However, for the salts having the same monovalent Cl−, Mg2+ has a greater hydration radius than Na+. Thus MgCl2 rejection is much higher than NaCl rejection due to the dominant size exclusion effect.

Under the neutral testing condition, the TFC-blank membrane has a more negatively charged surface than the TFN-3 membrane, but the TFN-3 membrane still exhibits slightly high salt rejections. On the one hand, AGQDs engaged in IP reaction lead to the chemical bondings between AGQDs and PA matrix, which enhances AGQDs's compatibility within the PA layer. On the other hand, the introduction of AGQDs in aqueous phase tends to attract more PIP on PSU support, which prefers to form a compact selective layer. Therefore, the weakening of Donnan exclusion effect could be compensated by the strengthening size exclusion effect.

The TFN-3 membrane was continuously tested for 12 h under a cross-flow filtration operation to investigate its stability. As illustrated in Fig. S2, the TFN-3 membrane maintains a high water flux as well as retains a stable salt rejection. It can be deduced that the introduction of inorganic AGQDs nanofillers enhances the mechanical stability of the TFN membrane, and the chemical linkages between AGQDs and PA matrix prevent the leakage of AGQDs from the selective layer during the long-term operation. Therefore, the AGQDs-incorporated TFN NF membrane exhibits the desirable long-term desalination performance.

Membrane fouling includes physical adsorption or chemical interaction between foulants and membrane surface, bringing about the temporary or permanent flux decline and the short service life. Herein, the antifouling ability is crucial for NF applications. It is widely accepted that the NF membrane with excellent hydrophilicity, neutral surface charge, and low surface roughness can strengthen the membrane's fouling resistance by forming the hydrated layer and/or weakening the interactions between foulants and membrane surface [46,51]. The antifouling performance of the selected NF membranes was evaluated by the normalized flux, using negatively charged BSA and positively charged lysozyme as the model foulants, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 11 , the normalized fluxes of both the TFC-blank and TFN-3 membranes present the declined tendency, but the normalized fluxes in the presence of lysozyme are much lower than the counterpart in the presence of BSA due to electrostatic attraction effect. Meanwhile, the TFC-blank membrane exhibits lower normalized fluxes than the TFN-3 membrane. It indicates that the foulants prefer to adhere to the rough and less hydrophilic TFC-blank membrane rather than the smooth and hydrophilic TFN-3 membrane. The thicker hydrated layer formed on the TFN-3 membrane could further intensify the tolerance of foulants by preventing foulants from contaminating membrane surfaces. At the neutral pH, the TFC-blank membrane possesses a stronger negative charge than the TFN-3 membrane. Thus the TFC-blank membrane generates the stronger electrostatic repulsion towards the negatively charged BSA and the stronger electrostatic attraction towards the positively charged lysozyme. Accordingly, it is expected to alleviate the membrane fouling in the presence of BSA and aggravate the membrane fouling in the presence of lysozyme. However, the TFN-3 membrane still exhibits a better antifouling performance than the TFC-blank membrane in the presence of BSA. It is suggested that the hydrophilic and smooth surface of the selective layer plays a crucial role in improving the membrane's fouling resistance in this study. At the same time, the relatively neutral charged surface of the TFN-3 membrane strengthens the tolerance towards differently charged foulants.

The PIP/TMC concentration ratio plays an essential role in salt rejections and water fluxes of the fabricated NF membranes. Usually, a high PIP concentration in aqueous phase results in a high salt rejection and a low water flux while using the same TMC concentration in the organic phase. According to the previous studies [13,52,53], two typical PIP/TMC ratios (10:1 and 1:1) are chosen to investigate the influences of AGQDs concentration on NF performance. As shown in Fig. S3, the impacts of AGQDs contents on NF performance under the high PIP/TMC ratio of 10:1 are less positive and attractive compared with those under the low PIP/TMC ratio of 1:1. Thus 0.1 wt% aqueous PIP monomers and 0.1 w/v% organic TMC monomers, corresponding to the PIP/TMC ratio of 1:1, are selected in this study.

Compared with the high PIP concentration (1 wt%), the low PIP concentration (0.1 wt%) produces a relatively thin and loose selective layer because fewer PIP monomers are available to react with TMC during the IP process. With the addition of AGQDs, more PIP monomers are enriched on PSU support, which has a more significant effect on the variation of monomer amount at the low PIP concentration. As a result, AGQDs plays a more substantial role in the TFN membrane made from low PIP concentration than that made from the high PIP concentration. Besides the steric hindrance effect, the introduction of AGQDs would generate the physical interactions with PIP monomers via hydrogen bond and electrostatic attraction (Fig. S4), which eventually affects the diffusion rate of PIP monomers and the reaction rate of the IP process.

According to Fig. 12 and Table S3, the optimal TFN-3 membrane exhibits superior permeaselectivity over the TFN NF membranes in other literature. For the PA TFC membranes, the higher water permeance is always at the expense of sacrificing salt rejection by reducing the cross-linking degree and/or the thickness of the selective layer. For the AGQDs-incorporated TFN membrane, an appropriate amount of AGQDs not only significantly reduces the membrane filtration resistance while maintaining the compact, integrated, and defect-free selective layer, but also enhances the membrane hydrophilicity and creates additional water channels. Therefore, the AGQDs-incorporated TFN membrane successfully overcomes the trade-off effect between water flux and salt rejection. Meanwhile, the introduction of AGQDs contributes to the formation of a smooth, hydrophilic, and electrically neutral surface, endowing the TFN membrane with excellent antifouling performance. This will promote the incorporated-TFN NF membrane to be used for water desalination with various foulants.

To develop the NF membrane with enhanced desalination and antifouling performance, L-Asp functionalized GQDs (AGQDs), taking advantage of L-Asp and GQDs, were successfully synthesized and incorporated into the selective layer via IP process in this study. The effects of AGQDs concentration on the NF structures, properties, desalination, and antifouling performance have been studied in-depth. With the addition of 3000 ppm AGQDs in aqueous solution, the optimal TFN-3 membrane has an excellent water permeance of 18.5 LMH/bar, 60.9% higher than the TFC-blank membrane. Its Na2SO4 rejection reaches 98.4%, which is also higher than that of the TFC-blank membrane (95.7%). Therefore, the trade-off effect between flux and rejection could be overwhelmed by introducing an appropriate amount of AGQDs, due to the synergistic effects of the lower filtration resistance, enhanced hydrophilicity, and additional water channels. Meanwhile, the TFN-3 membrane exhibits a stably long-term permeaselectivity. However, the addition of excessive AGQDs will reduce the cross-linking degree of selective layer, resulting in a decrease in salt rejection. In the presence of either the negatively charged foulant (BSA) or the positively charged foulant (lysozyme), the TFN-3 membrane demonstrates the superior antifouling performance over the TFC-blank membrane. The enhanced antifouling performance is ascribed to the formation of the smoother, more hydrophilic, and electrically neutral selective layer. Generally, the incorporation of amino acid-functionalized GQDs into the selective layer provides a novel strategy to develop a high-performance NF membrane, especially for the enhancement of desalination and antifouling performance.

Quanling Xie: Conceptualization, Writing-Reviewing and Editing, Supervision; Chenpu Wu: Experiment, Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation; Zhuan Hong: Experiment; Lufang Shen: Experiment; Tong Yu: Experiment; Honghui Guo: Experiment; Ying Xiong: Experiment; Guoliang Zhang: Software; Yinghua Lu : Supervision; Wenyao Shao : Supervision.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the Scientific Research Foundation of Third Institute of Oceanography, MNR (No. 2019012), the Fundamental Research Funds for Technology Planning Project of Xiamen City, China (No. 3502Z20183016), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21736009), the Science & Technology Planning Project of Fujian Province (No. 2019H0049), and Xiamen Ocean Research and Development Institute (No. K200103).
